text: This will be a discovery research interview, and the questions will be open-ended.
There are no right or wrong answers, so answer as honestly as you can.
Are you ready to get started?
Yep.
I'd like to talk about how you handle the data that comes back from your research.
When you finish an unmoderated study, what is the first thing you typically look at in the results?
For me, I normally have an idea when I'm setting up the study for the types of patterns I'm looking for.
So the first thing I would do is go and create those filters as a baseline, and that allows me to quickly, I guess, switch them on, switch them off, and measure the differences.
So let's say firstly I'm looking at people who answered A to question 1, and how they responded to question 2, and vice versa.
And I guess throughout that whole study, so I'm using it as a way to explore.
But ideally, what I've done recently is just export the CSV, put it into Claude Cowork, and work straight in there because it has great CSV analysis tools.
Could you walk me through a specific project where you exported that data into Claude?

Yeah, so I was doing an ideal customer profile piece of research to try and find who feels the most pain and is most motivated to change their ways of working for our industry streams product, where we own the research data and on-sell it to them.
So the research is already done before they get started.
So I was trying to learn what sorts of roles, what industries they were in, learning kind of what their current process is through an unmoderated study.
And then from there, I was trying to find those who were, I guess, most motivated to change and who are currently feeling the most pain by going in fairly blind.
Once you had that data in Claude, how did you use the tool to identify those specific people who are most motivated to change.

I had a question in there that was mostly around like showing a concept and like whether they would champion getting this rolled out within their organization or if they saw it more as a nice to have.
I've explained it poorly, but it that was one of the indicators.

The other ones were that they respect research, but another question asked what they're currently doing in their process and how they're— we found people were following their competitors blindly and they saw research as a hugely positive thing but also a bottleneck and they just didn't have the time to do it.
So there are a lot of questions that kind of amounted to this ICP.
How did it feel when you were going through that large amount of qualitative data to find those specific motivators?
Well, I didn't really have to do it because Claude did it for me.
So I gave it some guidance as to what I was looking for, from where we would see the value and trying to backtrack to find who the type of person is.
So it's kind of working back from a filter set, if that makes sense.
Aside from that ICP project, could you describe another specific instance where you had to synthesize unmoderated results into insights for your team?
Yeah, I did it a while back when I worked for Flight Centre.
Things, AI wasn't really popular then.
This was many years ago.

A good example of that one was it was a larger volume, more survey-based, but I had to basically work out firstly who was landing on our website and using the website, like what type of person they were, what segment they fitted into, and all that kind of thing.

And then from there, I had to learn more around their intent on the website, and that helped me establish then was the current version of our website serving the majority of the intent and found some pretty interesting things through that.
What specific steps did you take to turn those survey responses into the interesting things you shared with your team?
Yeah, so a lot of filtering of the data.

So once I segmented the different types of people, I was then looking at their intent and where they were at in their journey and found a mismatch between what they were trying to do and what they would have, I guess, required to— what information they were required to know, which they didn't know yet, that would then translate into, I guess, the next step of their travel journey.
So a good example was 25% of people didn't have an exact destination in mind, but that's the first field you have to fill out in a search.
And without doing that, you can't really progress through your journey.
So we didn't have the tools to help them decide on the best destination.
Same went for date.
That was half of people didn't have an exact date in mind, but you need exact dates to perform a search to get to the next stage.
So it was looking at that survey data and then looking at the baseline of what we had on the website.
And then trying to find the gaps.
Thank you so much for sharing those insights.
It's really helpful to see how your process has evolved from manual analysis to using AI.
Next, we're going to begin a website usability test, so I'll ask you to share your screen and then provide instructions for a specific task before opening the website in your browser.
Are you ready to move on to that part?
I've sent a link to your browser.
Please click the screen sharing button and choose to share this window.
I've got your screen share now.
Thank you.
For this next task, please review the results of the unmoderated test Participant App: Wallet Core Flows and Expectations on the Askable Product Team team.
As you go through, please describe your thoughts as you review the results at both the cohort and individual response level.
I'll open that page for you now.
The website is loading now.
Please remember to think aloud as you work through the task and just let me know when you feel you've completed it.
All right, could you please just remind me what the study was called that I'm looking for?
It's called Participant App: Wallet Core Flows and Expectations, and you're looking for it under the Askable Product Team team.
You can't seem to find that.
Let me just, uh, participant app wallet.
Okay, so it's not here.
I'm going to try and switch which team I'm working within.
Let's just try a different team, and then let me look here.
Participant app Wallet core flows and expectations.
Okay, I found it.
So the team provided was incorrect, but I did find how to switch teams.
So what I did was obviously searching was the quickest way to find that because the list is quite overwhelming, and then I'm going to click on that right now.
So sorry, what was the next part of the task that you had suggested?
No problem.
I'd like you to review the results of that test at both the cohort and individual response level, and just talk me through your thoughts as you look at them.
Okay, cool.
So I can see down the left that there are different blocks, which are the different tasks.
So firstly, I don't really know much about this study, so I'm just going to have a quick look around.

So I can't really see what this full screener is, which doesn't help.
I'm going to go back to recruit and look what that screener may have been.
Let's go down.

The answer is yes.
Okay, cool.
So that's what that screener is.
I'm going to go back to results.
It's not really helpful.
For me.
So I'm going to look at splitting this by demographic first.
So I mean, typically I would play around with that, but for the purpose of this, let's actually split by data.
So firstly, I'm probably going to look at people who find it important because this looks like something that we're considering doing.
So let's look at those 5 people who responded to that.
It looks like I can hover this, which looks clickable, but it doesn't appear to be clickable.
I would expect that to apply a filter, but let's go study block.
It's quite a bit of a workaround to get to this point.
So the rating is greater than or equal to 5.
I find this UI hard to read when a lot of the labels are cut off, but let's do this.
Okay, cool.
So we've now filtered to 5, so we can see those who found this the most important.
Let's look at some of the results that they have provided.
So when interacting with it, massive misclick rate on this.
Looks like a prototype test, it looks like a free explore because there's no goal completion.
When I go through here, I can see that there's quite a few different things Let me see.

Let's have a look at path 1.

So they were given a task.

That's fairly obvious.

From there, we can see that they ended up in the wallet, but it looks like they tried to click on the opportunity or the inbox there.
I feel like the wallet is behind here.

So that's helpful.
I can watch some of the recordings if I wanted to.
I won't do that now, but you can see clearly the screens that they were on.
I'm just going to close that.
Okay, so let's see what reasons for that these people would have felt it was important.
So minimum withdrawal, most of them are saying from that cohort are saying that it's less than 10.
Let's see how that would compare to all of them.
Okay, less than 10 is still the overarching opinion.
So it's interesting that that's such a low withdrawal limit.

Most studies paid out, particularly these who said that they would prefer to pool their money, are still saying like an amount that's the same or smaller than what would have currently been automatically paid.
So I wonder why they're saying they want to pool that money.
If that's the minimum withdrawal.
Maybe they just want that flexibility.
So let's have a look and see whether they, like, how else they want to use that.

So would you like to— okay, so most, I mean, it's fairly even, small numbers here, but most would prefer to let them build up and withdraw a sizable amount rather than withdraw Immediately, I feel like these people selected that 5, but then they're contradicting themselves a bit here, so I don't know how much I should trust it.
Oh, there you go, 50/50 split.
So that's fine.
Let's look a bit more into what was here.
Just having a look around, can see that this is another one.
It's more about the usability of finding the transaction history.
Looks like that was fairly straightforward for most people, though I'd probably look in here and look where they actually clicked.
First number I'd look at is that misclick rate, which is 75%.
So I don't think it's as easy as the numbers show.
I'd probably spend some time looking at where those issues are, what they're trying to click instead.
Okay, card sort.
So this is where would you expect to find the information?
Okay.
And it looks like there's— where's this similarity?
Okay, so they're connecting transaction history and taxes together quite a lot.
Taxes and withdrawal methods, transaction history and withdrawal methods.
So quite a bit there.
In terms of the agreement matrix, it looks like transaction history belongs in the wallet, so does taxes.
The completed studies is more in the my opportunities.
Okay, there's a lot of agreement in these, so I feel like that's a fairly straightforward thing.
Let's just hide that filter.

I would be typically applying a lot of different filters, but I'm not going to do that right now.
Okay, this one.
So, okay, I prefer my transaction history to show both financial details and the complete status.
Okay, so this is fairly split here.
13 of the 20 want it to show both details and the others don't want it.
Across the If you filter down to those who cared most about the wallet, looks like the financial details is the main focus there.
They also agreed on where it should live, so that makes sense.
I'm just going to skip through these last parts just to see what the overarching withdrawal methods would be like.

I wish this was a bit clearer, but consistently expressed strong preference for direct bank transfers as a withdrawal method.
Okay, 35% of responses.

Gaming, crypto.
Um, yeah, it's quite a broad range.
This is good.

Um, okay, I think that's it.
I think I'm done with the task.
Thank you for working through that.
Shall we move on to the next part?
Yeah, let's go.
When you were navigating through the results for the Wallet Core Flows Study, what was the first thing that made you feel like the interface was slowing you down?
The first one was that the labels were not showing the entire copy and there was no way to really see that.
I had to go to a different view to see the screener question complete label, which I think is a big flaw.
Additionally, even like the system labels were cut off and truncated, so that really slowed me down.
I think the jumping between, like, too much navigation.
So I was applying filters but then also jumping between the blocks.
I couldn't just like scroll to see the difference or collapse different result sets.
I like to have everything in one view to make it a lot clearer.
I also think at one point, I didn't say this out loud, but I did notice that I got confused as to whether the filter was turned on or off, knowing that I could show and hide that thing.
It was on, I realized, but the state kind of confused me a little bit as well.
That sounds frustrating, especially when you're trying to stay in a flow.
Could you walk me through the specific steps you took when you had to leave your current view just to read those truncated labels?
Yeah, so I went— I know the product, so I went to Recruit to see the table, and then I could hover over the column heading and that showed it in a tooltip.
That actually helped.
The other options, I don't know what I would do.
If it was a task, I guess I'd click on the task and go read that.
It does make it pretty hard to manage though.
Yeah, I think it's more of an interface problem there.
How did that feeling of confusion about whether the filter was on or off actually impact your confidence in the data you were looking at?
Um, it didn't really impact it because I just quickly toggled it to see, and it was obvious once you see it again.
But then if you navigate away from it, you kind of forget what state it's in.
And it's, I guess, the The current state is not super obvious if you don't know the other state.
So it's like more of a comparative thing that makes it easier.
Aside from the truncated labels and filter states, what specific aspect of configuring the charts or visualizations felt most cumbersome to you?
Oh yeah, that was one.
I expected to just be able to click on a graph.
It shows a hover state, so I would expect to be able to just click that to be like, I want to 'filter on this particular thing,' and it would add it to my filters list.

So I think doing it on the side, when you have to not only— like, I was already on the task, and then I go to apply the filter, and then I had to go and find the task again in that list, and then select it, and then select which option, when that all could have been done with just a single click on the bar itself.
When you realized you had to manually find that task again in the sidebar list instead of just clicking the graph, what was going through your mind?
Oh, just that it was cumbersome.
And then that's when I got lost to know which task it was as well, because it was truncated.
So it had flow-on effects of confusion.
Whereas, I don't know, if you clicked it and then it just showed up right beside you, Thank you so much for walking me through your process today.
Your insights into how you use AI for analysis and your feedback on the filtering experience were incredibly valuable.
